content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 24, 2016

Michael Dukes
244 Samantha Court
Imperial, CA 92251

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-052

Dear Mr. Dukes:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-employment restrictions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that our advice is based solely on the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application of other post-government employment law. Also, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission",) does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you from working in direct sales, general procurement, and contract support for a supplier of goods to state agencies where you previously worked for the California Department of Correction and Rehabilitation ("CDCR") as a Material & Stores Supervisor 1 at its Centinela facility?

CONCLUSION

No. The permanent and one-year bans found in the Act's restrictions on post-governmental employment will not apply to your proposed employment because your state position was not, nor should it have been, designated in the CDCR's conflict of interest code.

FACTS

On January 29, 2016 you retired from state service as a Material & Stores Supervisor at the CDCR's Centinela facility. Your primary duties included inventory control, stock rotation, stock ordering, and receiving. Only a portion of your job duties included interacting with vendors selling goods to the state. You were responsible for keeping the warehouse stocked and, therefore, made purchases as needed to replenish stock. All purchases were made in accordance with strict guidelines under a purchase agreement. You did not participate in any contract negotiations or

___________________

The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-052
Page No. 2

execution. In making these purchases, you were required to follow the guidelines and select only
those vendors that were on an approved vendor list. You did not participate in pre-qualification or
selection of vendors for the list. These duties were performed by employees in authority positions
several levels above yours. You state that you were not required under the CDCR's conflict of
interest code to file a Form 700 "Statement of Economic Interests."

You now wish to accept employment with Elite Supply Source which is a supplier of goods
to a number of state agencies. Your role would include direct sales as well as general procurement
and contract support.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions
are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions.

One-Year Ban

The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section 87406; Regulation
18746.1.)

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code.
(Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).)

An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Ibid.)

Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state
agency the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "which budgets
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and control of the
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6).)

File No. A-16-052
Page No. 3

Permanent Ban

The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating, for compensation, in certain proceeding involving the State of California and other
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of
absence from any particular office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(1).)

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any
oral or written communication – or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or
communication - made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. "'Judicial, quasi-
judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . . .
.." (Section 87400(c).)

Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of
confidential information . . .." (Section 87400(d).)

You state that your former position was not designated in the CDCR's conflict of interest
code. Your description of the facts further indicates that your authority to purchase goods was
carefully circumscribed. You were only permitted to purchase goods for the purpose of restocking
existing inventory. You could only select vendors from the approved vendor list and you had no
role whatsoever in establishing that list. Based on your very limited authority to purchase goods, the
work you are proposing at Elite Supply Source is not prohibited by either the one-year ban or the
permanent ban.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions 
are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 
One-Year Ban 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her

discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,

18746.1.) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406( d)( I); Regulation 18746.1 (a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date 
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban 
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.I(b)(3) and (4).) 
An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the 
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication

Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve

Permanent Ban  
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in certain proceeding involving the State of California and other

The pennanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. '"Judicial, quasi­
judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other 
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other 
particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . .  
.. " (Section 87400(c).) 
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she 
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,
